name: Taṇhaṅkara
description: The first of three Buddhas who arose in the same sāra-maṇḍa-kappa as Dīpaṅkara, preceding him within that aeon, before the city of Amaravatī existed. The placement shown is a display position, not a counted aeon-figure.
significance: The Buddhavaṃsa preserves only his name; the full chronicle is not recorded. At this depth the future Gotama was aspiring to Buddhahood in mind alone (mano-paṇidhi), not yet able to receive a spoken prophecy.

name: Medhaṅkara
description: The second Buddha of that same sāra-maṇḍa-kappa preceding Dīpaṅkara, likewise recorded by name alone. The placement shown is a display position, not a counted aeon-figure.
significance: By his era the aspiration of the future Gotama had deepened from thought into speech (mano- and vaci-paṇidhi), though he was not yet endowed with the eight factors required to receive a definite prophecy.

name: Saraṇaṅkara
description: The third of the three Buddhas of that aeon, arising immediately before Dīpaṅkara; an intermediate period (antara-kappa) separated them. The placement shown is a display position, not a counted aeon-figure.
significance: He closes the stretch of the lineage recorded by name only. In the next Buddha-era the same aspirant, reborn as the ascetic Sumedha, receives the first definite prophecy.

name: Dīpaṅkara
description: The Buddha of the city of Rammavatī, eighty cubits in height, with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened beneath a pepper (pipphali) tree. The tradition places him four asaṅkhyeyyas and 100,000 aeons before the present; the chart position is a display value, since an asaṅkhyeyya is not a countable number of aeons.
significance: The turning point of the lineage: seeing him approach over muddy ground, the ascetic Sumedha lay down as a bridge and resolved to forgo his own imminent liberation, receiving the first definite prophecy of Buddhahood and beginning the four asaṅkhyeyyas and 100,000 aeons of fulfilment.

name: Dīpaṅkara's Declaration
description: Dīpaṅkara Buddha, reading Sumedha's mind, halted and declared before gods and men that this ascetic would, four incalculable periods and a hundred thousand aeons thence, become a Buddha named Gotama.
significance: The tradition holds that an aspiration becomes a settled bodhisatta career only when a living Buddha confirms it aloud; Dīpaṅkara is accordingly counted first of the twenty-four Buddhas who gave the prophecy.

name: Energy
description: The perfection of unrelenting effort that never sinks into despair — effort despite fatigue, despite failure, and despite the loss of all companions and hope — exemplified by Prince Mahājanaka, shipwrecked mid-ocean, who swam seven days toward a shore he could not see.
significance: Sustained across the career, it is the quality that makes a path measured in incalculable aeons endurable at all.

name: Equanimity
description: The perfection of even-mindedness — balance in ordinary ups and downs, then under honour and contempt, then held level as a weighing-scale toward all beings always — exemplified by the Great Being of 'Bristling Hair', undisturbed alike by the mockery of village boys and the honours of the wise.
significance: Listed last and sustained across the career, it keeps the other nine perfections free of preference between friend and foe.

name: Forbearance
description: The perfection of patient endurance without resentment, graded from bearing harsh words to bearing physical harm to bearing mutilation with loving calm, exemplified by Khantivādī the recluse, who felt no anger as King Kalābu cut off his hands and feet and who wished the king well.
significance: Sustained across the career, it shows patience as a trained capacity rather than a temperament, its supreme grade holding non-hatred under bodily mutilation.

name: Four Incalculables and a Hundred Thousand Aeons
description: The length of Gotama's bodhisatta career as the texts state it: four asaṅkheyyas — periods of aeons the tradition declares uncountable — together with a further hundred thousand mahākappas of continuous perfection-building.
significance: An asaṅkheyya is not a stated quantity; the word means 'not to be counted'. The duration of the path is therefore given in a unit the tradition itself places beyond reckoning.

name: Generosity
description: The perfection of relinquishing, graded as giving external wealth (ordinary), giving one's own limbs (higher), and giving one's very life (supreme), exemplified by the Wise Hare who leapt into a blazing fire to feed a beggar — Sakka in disguise — having nothing else to offer.
significance: Cultivated across the whole career rather than at any dated moment; the three grades measure each perfection by how much of oneself is given, not by the size of the gift.

name: Loving-Kindness
description: The perfection of boundless goodwill, extended first to the pleasant, then to the neutral, then to those who would take one's life, exemplified by Suvaṇṇasāma, who lived in such radiant kindness toward all creatures that wild animals walked beside him, and who maintained it even as he was struck down.
significance: Widened by degrees across the whole career until no being falls outside it.

name: Morality
description: The perfection of guarding conduct, kept though one lose property (ordinary), limbs (higher), or life (supreme), exemplified by Saṅkhapāla the nāga king who was pierced at eight places by sixteen hunters and bore it without anger rather than break his precepts.
significance: Cultivated across the whole career rather than at a dated occurrence; its supreme grade sets virtue above the preservation of one's own life.

name: Renunciation
description: The perfection of letting go of sense-pleasures and the household life, graded from renouncing possessions to renouncing status and family ties to renouncing the throne itself, fearless of loss, exemplified by King Cūḷa Sutasoma who abdicated Bārāṇasī to seek the deathless.
significance: Sustained across the career rather than fixed to a date, it records a disposition rehearsed life after life before it could be complete in the final one.

name: Resolution
description: The perfection of unshakable determination — a vow held despite discomfort, then despite opposition, then at the risk of life — exemplified by Prince Temiya, who held a vow of feigned deaf-mute stillness for sixteen years to escape a throne that would have forced him into cruelty.
significance: Sustained across the career, it is the perfection that carries Sumedha's original vow unbroken across four incalculable periods.

name: Sumedha's Vow at the Feet of Dīpaṅkara
description: The ascetic Sumedha, who could have burnt away every defilement under Dīpaṅkara Buddha that very day and entered Nibbāna as a disciple, instead spread his body, his matted hair and his animal-skin robe across the mire as a bridge for the Buddha and his arahats to cross, and vowed to become a fully self-awakened Buddha himself for the sake of all beings.
significance: This deliberate choice — to set aside liberation already within reach, for four incalculable periods and a hundred thousand aeons — is the origin point of the bodhisatta career that ends with Gotama.

name: The Bodhisatta Career
description: The whole span from Sumedha's vow to the awakening under the Bodhi tree at Uruvelā, lived out across innumerable births as human, animal and deva while the ten perfections were brought to maturity.
significance: It places the Buddha's awakening not at the end of one lifetime of striving but at the end of a labour longer, in this cosmology, than the age of the physical universe as science measures it.

name: The Eight Conditions of the Aspiration
description: Eight factors that had to coincide for Sumedha's aspiration to be valid: human birth, male sex, sufficient maturity to attain arahantship in that life, the presence of a living Buddha, the going-forth as an ascetic, attainment of the jhānas and higher knowledges, an act of extreme sacrifice, and unwavering resolve.
significance: The tradition treats the beginning of a Buddha's career as vanishingly rare — a conjunction of eight conditions in a universe where a living Buddha is itself an event separated from the next by thousands of aeons.

name: The Eight Factors of the Aspiration
description: Eight factors that had to coincide for Sumedha's aspiration to succeed: human birth, male sex, sufficient maturity to attain arahantship in that life, the presence of a living Buddha, the going-forth as an ascetic, attainment of the jhānas and higher knowledges, an act of extreme sacrifice, and unwavering desire for Awakening.
significance: The tradition treats the beginning of a Buddha's career as exceedingly rare — a conjunction of eight factors in a world where the arising of a living Buddha is itself separated from the next by many aeons.

name: The Ten Perfections
description: The complete set of ten qualities — generosity, morality, renunciation, wisdom, energy, forbearance, truthfulness, resolution, loving-kindness and equanimity — each carrying three grades, ordinary, higher (at the cost of limb) and supreme (at the cost of life), brought to completion over the whole bodhisatta career.
significance: Thirty grades of perfection give the timescale its purpose: the aeons are not spent waiting but in the work of bringing each quality to completion.

name: Truthfulness
description: The perfection of absolute reliability of word — truth in small things, then kept at the cost of wealth, then at the cost of life — exemplified by King Mahā Sutasoma, who returned to the cannibal Porisāda as he had promised and by that truth saved a hundred and one kings.
significance: Sustained across the career rather than fixed to a date; a Buddha's teaching rests on the reliability of what he says, and this perfection is what makes that reliability unconditional.

name: Wisdom
description: The perfection of discernment — seeing things as they truly are and acting on it — rising from worldly discernment through insight into cause and effect to the wisdom that liberates self and others, exemplified by the wise Senaka who perceived a hidden cobra coiled inside a leather food-bag and saved a brahmin's life.
significance: Ripened over the whole career as a faculty rather than learned as a doctrine; its highest grade is wisdom that frees not oneself alone but others as well.

name: Koṇḍañña
description: The sole Buddha of his aeon, eighty-eight cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened under a beautiful sāla tree (sāla-kalyāṇī) at Rammavatī. His distance from Dīpaṅkara is given as an immeasurable interval, not a count of aeons; the chart position is a display value.
significance: A Buddhantara-asaṅkhyeyya — an immeasurable interval — separates him from Dīpaṅkara. In his time the Bodhisatta was the universal monarch Vijitāvī, who offered great alms and then gave up his empire to ordain.

name: Aeon of Four Buddhas
description: An aeon in which four Buddhas arise, as Maṅgala, Sumana, Revata and Sobhita did. The aeon of Taṇhaṅkara, Medhaṅkara, Saraṇaṅkara and Dīpaṅkara was of this same kind.
significance: The aeon in which our Bodhisatta received his first definite prophecy, from Dīpaṅkara, was itself one of these rare four-Buddha cycles.

name: Maṅgala
description: First of four Buddhas to arise in a single sāra-maṇḍa-kappa, eighty-eight cubits tall with a 90,000-year lifespan, awakened beneath an ironwood (nāga) tree at Uttara. The chart position is a display value; the tradition reckons the distance in asaṅkhyeyyas.
significance: His bodily radiance is said to have filled ten thousand world-systems, brighter than that of any other Buddha. In his time the Bodhisatta was the brahmin Suruci, who took refuge and made a great offering of food.

name: Sumana
description: Second Buddha of that same four-Buddha aeon, ninety cubits tall with a lifespan of 90,000 years, awakened under an ironwood (nāga) tree at Mekhala. The offset from Maṅgala is a display spacing within one aeon, not a recorded interval.
significance: Four Buddhas sharing one aeon is rare in a cosmology where most aeons pass with none at all. In his era the Bodhisatta was the nāga king Atula, who honoured him with celestial music and offerings.

name: Revata
description: Third Buddha of that same aeon, eighty cubits tall with a lifespan of 60,000 years, awakened under an ironwood (nāga) tree at Sudhaññavatī. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: His is the shortest lifespan of the four Buddhas of that aeon. In his time the Bodhisatta was the brahmin Atideva, who took refuge, praised the Buddha in a thousand verses, and offered his upper garment.

name: Sobhita
description: Fourth and last Buddha of that aeon, fifty-eight cubits tall with a 90,000-year lifespan, awakened beneath an ironwood (nāga) tree at Sudhamma. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: With his passing the four-Buddha aeon ends, and an empty asaṅkhyeyya (suñña-kappa) follows in which no Buddha appears. In his time the Bodhisatta was the brahmin Sujāta, who gave alms through the three-month Rains Retreat.

name: The Empty Aeon
description: An aeon in which no Buddha arises, no Dhamma is taught and no path is available. After Buddha Sobhita an entire incalculable stretch of such aeons passed before Anomadassī appeared.
significance: Empty aeons far outnumber the others, so the presence of a teaching in one's own lifetime is the exception and not the rule.

name: Anomadassī
description: First of three Buddhas arising after an empty asaṅkhyeyya, fifty-eight cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened under an arjuna (ajjuna) tree at Candavatī. The chart position is a display value; the interval is reckoned in asaṅkhyeyyas.
significance: An immeasurable stretch passed with no Buddha before him. Under his teaching the beings who would become Sāriputta and Moggallāna first aspired to be chief disciples, and the Bodhisatta, then a yakkha general, received the prophecy of Buddhahood one asaṅkhyeyya and 100,000 aeons hence.

name: Paduma
description: Second Buddha of that same aeon, fifty-eight cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened beneath a great soṇa (mahā-soṇa) tree in the Campaka grove. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: In his era the Bodhisatta was a lion who guarded the Buddha while he was absorbed in the attainment of cessation, standing watch seven days without food — the perfections may be gathered even in animal birth.

name: Nārada
description: Third Buddha of that same aeon, eighty-eight cubits tall with a 90,000-year lifespan, awakened under a great soṇa (mahā-soṇa) tree at Dhaññavatī. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: Born of the line of universal monarchs, he was honoured by the Bodhisatta, then a matted-hair recluse of the Himālayas, who hosted him and his disciples for seven days and offered red sandalwood.

name: Padumuttara
description: The sole Buddha of his aeon, 100,000 aeons before the present, fifty-eight cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened beneath a salala tree at Haṃsavatī.
significance: Nearly all eighty of Gotama's foremost disciples are said to have made their original aspirations under his Dispensation. After his aeon, seventy thousand aeons passed with no Buddha at all — the longest recorded silence in the lineage. In his time the Bodhisatta was the wealthy governor Jaṭila.

name: Sumedha
description: One of two Buddhas of a maṇḍa-kappa 30,000 aeons before the present, eighty-eight cubits tall with a 90,000-year lifespan, awakened under a great nīpa (asoka) tree at Sudassana.
significance: He is not to be confused with Sumedha the ascetic who received the prophecy under Dīpaṅkara. In his time that same aspirant, now the youth Uttara, gave away a fortune of 800 million and ordained.

name: Sujāta
description: Second Buddha of that same maṇḍa-kappa 30,000 aeons ago, fifty cubits tall with a lifespan of 90,000 years, awakened beneath a great bamboo (mahā-veḷu) at Sumaṅgala. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: In his era the Bodhisatta was a universal monarch who offered his kingship and the seven treasures of a world-emperor to the Buddha and Saṅgha, then became a monk.

name: Aeon of Three Buddhas
description: An aeon in which three Buddhas arise, as Piyadassī, Atthadassī and Dhammadassī did some eighteen hundred aeons ago.
significance: Three Dispensations within one world-cycle is called excellent (vara) because it is so seldom the case.

name: Piyadassī
description: First of three Buddhas of a vara-kappa 1,800 aeons ago, eighty cubits tall with a 90,000-year lifespan, awakened under a kakudha tree at Sudhaññavatī.
significance: In his time the Bodhisatta was the brahmin youth Kassapa, who built a monastery for the Saṅgha at great cost and kept the five precepts steadfastly, receiving the prophecy that he would become a Buddha 1,800 aeons hence.

name: Atthadassī
description: Second Buddha of that same vara-kappa 1,800 aeons ago, eighty cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened beneath a campak tree at Sobhana. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: In his time the Bodhisatta was the ascetic Susīma, a wealthy brahmin who first gave away all he had to the poor, then brought celestial flowers from the Tāvatiṃsa heaven in homage as he listened to the Dhamma.

name: Dhammadassī
description: Third Buddha of that same vara-kappa 1,800 aeons ago, eighty cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened under a bimbijāla tree at Saraṇa. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: The Bodhisatta met him as Sakka, lord of the Tāvatiṃsa heaven, coming with the devas of two heavens to honour him — in this cosmology even the king of the devas is still a being on the path, not one who has finished it.

name: Aeon of One Buddha
description: An aeon graced by a single Buddha, as with Siddhattha ninety-four aeons ago, and with Koṇḍañña and Padumuttara before him.
significance: The lowest grade of non-empty aeon, and already rare against the background of empty ones.

name: Siddhattha
description: The one and only Buddha of his aeon, 94 aeons before the present, sixty cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened beneath a kaṇikāra tree at Vebhāra.
significance: In his time the Bodhisatta was the ascetic Maṅgala, who offered rose-apple fruit brought by psychic power and was told he would become a Buddha in the ninety-fourth aeon from then. Human life in that age is reckoned at 100,000 years, against a hundred in ours.

name: Aeon of Two Buddhas
description: An aeon in which two Buddhas arise, as Tissa and Phussa did ninety-two aeons ago, and Sumedha and Sujāta some thirty thousand aeons ago.
significance: Each additional Buddha in an aeon marks a further degree of rarity in the tradition's grading of cosmic time.

name: Tissa
description: First of two Buddhas of a maṇḍa-kappa 92 aeons ago, ten cubits tall with a lifespan of 100,000 years, awakened under an Indian laurel (asana) tree at Khemaka.
significance: Described as standing like a Himalayan peak despite the smallest recorded stature in the lineage. In his time the Bodhisatta was King Sujāta of Yasavatī, who had renounced his throne and held a lotus-sunshade over the Buddha before the assembly.

name: Phussa
description: Second Buddha of that same maṇḍa-kappa 92 aeons ago, fifty-eight cubits tall with a 90,000-year lifespan, awakened beneath an emblic myrobalan (āmalaka) tree at Kāsi. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: In his time the Bodhisatta was King Vijitāvī of Arindama, who gave away his city, ordained, and mastered and taught the texts. The commentaries set the Tirokuḍḍa story of the hungry ghosts in his Dispensation, their relatives making offerings for them under Gotama ninety-two aeons later.

name: Vipassī
description: The Buddha of 91 aeons ago, eighty cubits tall with a lifespan of 80,000 years, awakened under a trumpet-flower (pāṭali) tree at Bandhumatī.
significance: His life is narrated at length in the Mahāpadāna Sutta, making him the earliest predecessor described in full in the discourses rather than in verse lists alone. In his time the Bodhisatta was again the nāga king Atula, who gave seven days of alms and offered a golden throne.

name: Destruction by Fire, Water and Wind
description: World-systems are destroyed in a repeating pattern of sixty-four. Seven destructions by fire sweep away everything up to the Brahmā plane, sparing the Ābhassara; the eighth, by water, carries away the Ābhassara as well and spares the Subhakiṇha. After seven such rounds and seven further fires, the sixty-fourth destruction is by wind, which carries away the Subhakiṇha and leaves only the Vehapphala and above untouched.
significance: The pattern makes plain that even the long-lived brahmā heavens are impermanent: a high rebirth postpones the ending, it does not escape it.

name: Sikhī
description: First of two Buddhas arising 31 aeons ago, seventy cubits tall with a lifespan of 70,000 years, awakened beneath a white mango (puṇḍarīka) tree at Aruṇavatī.
significance: Fifty-nine empty aeons, with no Buddha and no teaching, preceded him — emptiness is the ordinary state of the cosmos and the arising of a Buddha the exception. In his time the Bodhisatta was King Arindama, who gave his own state elephant to the Buddha and Saṅgha.

name: Vessabhū
description: Second Buddha of that same aeon 31 aeons ago, sixty cubits tall with a lifespan of 60,000 years, awakened under a great sāla tree at Anoma. The offset shown is a display spacing within one aeon.
significance: He is the last Buddha before the present aeon. In his time the Bodhisatta, as King Sudassana of Sarabhavatī, built a dwelling for the Buddha and huts for the Saṅgha, gave away his royal wealth, ordained, and kept the thirteen ascetic practices, still seeking omniscience in the closing stretch of the long fulfilment.

name: The Twenty-Nine Empty Aeons
description: The stretch of aeons between Vessabhū, last Buddha of the thirty-first aeon back, and Kakusandha, first of the present one, in which no Buddha arose and no teaching was heard.
significance: An aeon in which no Buddha appears is called empty, and twenty-nine of them in unbroken sequence is the ordinary condition of the cosmos rather than the exception.

name: The Measure of an Aeon
description: The unit on which this whole timeline is drawn: when asked how long an aeon lasts, the Buddha declined to give a number of years and offered two similes — a mountain worn away by a silk cloth stroked once a century, and a city of mustard seeds emptied one seed at a time.
significance: In both similes the mountain wears away and the seeds run out before the aeon ends, so any figure derived from them is a floor rather than a measurement.

name: The Auspicious Aeon
description: The rarest grade of aeon, in which five Buddhas arise. The present one has already seen Kakusandha, Koṇāgamana, Kassapa and Gotama, with Metteyya still to come.
significance: We stand inside the most fortunate kind of world-cycle the cosmology describes, with one Buddha of it still ahead.

name: Dissolution
description: The first of the four incalculable phases of a great aeon, in which the world-system contracts and is destroyed. The commentaries add that beings are meanwhile reborn in the higher realms the destruction does not reach.
significance: Dissolution is described as gradual and lawful rather than as judgement: worlds end as everything conditioned ends.

name: Modern Year-Estimates of an Aeon
description: Modern writers sometimes work the two similes into year-figures. The results diverge enormously, because the size of a mustard seed and the rate at which cloth wears rock are supplied by the calculator, not by the texts.
significance: Such figures are arithmetic laid over a simile. The suttas give no number for a kappa, and this timeline offers none.

name: The Great Aeon
description: The mahākappa is the full life-cycle of a world-system, running from its dissolution through its long emptiness, its re-evolution, and the long stretch during which it stands and beings live in it.
significance: It is the basic unit of Buddhist deep time, and the whole lineage of the twenty-nine Buddhas is measured in hundreds of thousands of them.

name: The Refusal to Count the Years
description: Asked directly how long an aeon lasts, the Buddha replied that it is not easy to reckon it as so many years, so many hundreds of years, so many thousands or hundreds of thousands of years.
significance: Every year-figure attached to a kappa, including any on this timeline, is a later human calculation and not scripture.

name: The Simile of the Mountain
description: A solid rock mountain a league high, wide and deep, without cleft or crevice, and a man who strokes it once every hundred years with a fine cloth of Kāsi: the mountain would wear away before the aeon ended.
significance: Having declined to give a number, the Buddha gave a measure by image instead.

name: The Simile of the Mustard Seeds
description: An iron-walled city a league on each side heaped with mustard seeds, and a man who removes a single seed every hundred years: the heap would be exhausted before the aeon ended.
significance: A second image for the same span, given in place of an arithmetic the question could not be answered with.

name: The Dissolved Aeon
description: The second phase, during which the world-system remains dissolved: an immense duration in which there is nothing where a world was.
significance: A full quarter of the longest unit in the cosmology is given over to absence.

name: Evolution
description: The third phase, in which the world-system re-forms. The Aggañña Sutta describes beings descending from the Ābhassara brahmā world as their store of merit is exhausted; the ordering of the realms as they reappear is set out in the commentaries.
significance: Worlds are described as re-arising by natural process out of past action, with no creator required.

name: The Evolved Aeon
description: The fourth phase, in which the world stands complete and inhabited. The tradition holds that it is only during this phase that Buddhas arise and the Dhamma can be taught.
significance: The window for awakening is a fraction of cosmic time, which is why a human birth while a Dispensation stands is called rare.

name: The Incalculable Aeon
description: An aggregate of great aeons so large that the tradition declines to number it, used only for the longest reckonings, above all the four asaṅkhyeyyas and a hundred thousand aeons of the Bodhisatta's course.
significance: Placed here only to mark the far edge of the chart; its true magnitude is not a number the timeline can honestly hold.

name: The Intermediate Aeon
description: A sub-cycle within the evolved phase, measured by the swing of the human lifespan. DN 26 traces it falling from eighty thousand years to ten and climbing back again; the commentaries count sixty-four such cycles in the fourth phase of a great aeon.
significance: It supplies the rhythm by which Buddhas are spaced: Kakusandha arose at a lifespan of forty thousand years, Koṇāgamana at thirty thousand, Kassapa at twenty thousand, and Gotama at about a hundred.

name: The Great Passing
description: The death of Gotama at Kusinārā in his eightieth year, with the final instruction that all conditioned things are subject to decay.
significance: By traditional Theravāda reckoning this falls around 544 BCE and anchors the Buddhist Era, though modern scholarship argues for a later date; on the aeon's scale it and the awakening are a single indistinguishable point.

name: The Turning of the Wheel
description: Gotama's first discourse, given to the five ascetics at Isipatana near Bārāṇasī, setting out the middle way and the four noble truths.
significance: This is the point at which the Dhamma becomes public in this aeon.

name: Kakusandha
description: The first Buddha of the present Auspicious Aeon, born into a brahmin family at Khemavatī and awakened beneath an acacia (sirīsa) tree when the human lifespan stood at 40,000 years.
significance: His arising ends twenty-nine consecutive empty aeons in which no Buddha appeared at all, and opens the rare aeon that will hold five.

name: Koṇāgamana
description: The second Buddha of this aeon, awakened beneath a cluster-fig (udumbara) tree at Sobhavatī when the human lifespan had fallen to 30,000 years.
significance: The descending lifespans from Kakusandha to Gotama — 40,000, 30,000, 20,000, 100 — mark the aeon's own slow decline as plainly as any calendar.

name: Kassapa
description: The third Buddha of this aeon, awakened beneath a banyan (nigrodha) tree at Bārāṇasī when the human lifespan was 20,000 years; the Buddha immediately preceding Gotama.
significance: Under his teaching the Bodhisatta, then the brahmin youth Jotipāla, ordained and trained in morality, concentration and wisdom — the last such training before his own Buddhahood.

name: Gotama
description: The fourth Buddha of this aeon — Siddhattha Gotama of the Sakyans — awakened beneath the Assattha tree at Bodh Gayā when the human lifespan was about a hundred years, and taught for forty-five years.
significance: Here the vow made before Dīpaṅkara four asaṅkhyeyyas and 100,000 aeons earlier is fulfilled, and the whole preceding lineage stands complete.

name: Gotama's Dispensation
description: The span during which Gotama's teaching, practice and attainment remain available in the world, which the commentaries reckon at five thousand years from the Parinibbāna.
significance: Five thousand years is the widest window the commentarial tradition claims, and against a single mahā-kappa it is too brief to be drawn at all.

name: Beautiful Devas
description: The third of the Pure Abodes, a fine-material plane open only to non-returners.
significance: The Pure Abodes double in stated lifespan at each ascent — 1,000, 2,000, 4,000, 8,000, 16,000 mahākappas — mapping degrees of maturity onto an ordered scale.

name: Clear-Sighted Devas
description: The fourth of the Pure Abodes, inhabited by non-returners established in the fourth jhāna; the name is glossed as clear-seeing.
significance: Eight thousand world-cycles, a reminder that even attainment close to the final goal still unfolds across immense stretches of time.

name: Devas Delighting in Creation
description: The fifth sense-sphere heaven, whose devas create the objects of their own enjoyment, living 8,000 celestial years — given as 2,304,000,000 human years.
significance: A span of over two billion human years that still ends within a fraction of one world-cycle. The value shown ranks the heavens rather than measuring them.

name: Devas Not Falling Away
description: The lowest of the five Pure Abodes, where non-returners are reborn; the name is traditionally glossed as not falling away from their attainment.
significance: A thousand world-cycles is the shortest of the Pure Abodes and still exceeds the lifespan of every heaven of sense pleasure.

name: Devas of Limited Glory
description: The lowest third-jhāna plane of the fine-material world.
significance: Sixteen mahākappas is a modest span among the Brahmā worlds and an unimaginable one from any human vantage.

name: Devas of Limited Radiance
description: The lowest second-jhāna plane of the fine-material world.
significance: Two world-cycles is the smallest lifespan the lists give as a whole number of mahākappas; below this the first-jhāna planes are reckoned in incalculable aeons instead.

name: Devas of Refulgent Glory
description: The highest third-jhāna plane of the fine-material world, suffused with steady radiance.
significance: Its span matches the sixty-four-cycle interval at which the Visuddhimagga places destruction by wind, the one destruction that reaches this plane.

name: Devas of Streaming Radiance
description: The highest second-jhāna plane, whose beings are described as made of mind and feeding on joy.
significance: The Aggañña Sutta says that when the world contracts, beings are for the most part reborn here, and descend again as the world re-evolves.

name: Devas of the Four Great Kings
description: The lowest heaven, guarded by the four great kings, where one celestial day equals fifty human years and life lasts 500 celestial years — given as 9,000,000 human years.
significance: Nine million years is the entry rung of the heavens; measured against a mahākappa it is negligible.

name: Devas of Unbounded Glory
description: The middle third-jhāna plane of the fine-material world.
significance: Thirty-two world-cycles, one grade below the highest third-jhāna plane and one above the lowest.

name: Devas of Unbounded Radiance
description: The middle second-jhāna plane of the fine-material world.
significance: Four mahākappas is short by the reckoning of these planes and still spans four full formations and dissolutions of a world.

name: Devas Wielding Power over Others' Creations
description: The highest of the six sense-sphere heavens, where beings enjoy pleasures fashioned for them by others, with a lifespan of 16,000 celestial years — given in the source as 9,216,000,000 human years.
significance: The longest sensual-heaven life, yet small against a single mahākappa. The plotted value is a placeholder on an assumed scale, since the texts give no year-length for a mahākappa.

name: Great Brahmās
description: The highest first-jhāna plane, whose lifespan the texts give as one incalculable aeon (asaṅkheyya-kappa) rather than a counted number.
significance: An incalculable is traditionally one of the four immeasurable phases of a mahākappa, so the span is plotted here at a quarter cycle — a proportion inferred for ordering, since the texts decline to count it.

name: Human Beings
description: The human plane, where lifespan is not fixed. It stands at about a hundred years now; the Cakkavatti-sīhanāda Sutta describes it contracting to ten years and expanding to eighty thousand, while the commentaries extend the upper range to the incalculable.
significance: Brief and unstable, yet the one plane in which the perfections can be actively built and in which a Buddha arises. No fixed endpoint can be plotted for it.

name: Hungry Ghosts / Shades
description: A woeful plane arising from greed, miserliness and wrong view, with no fixed lifespan, though often described as very long.
significance: No endpoint can be plotted, because the duration is determined by kamma rather than by any stated span for the realm.

name: Infinite Consciousness
description: The second formless realm, attained through absorption on the boundlessness of consciousness itself.
significance: The formless spans descend in uneven steps — 84,000, 60,000, 40,000, 20,000 — so the grades are ranked without following any single arithmetic ratio.

name: Infinite Space
description: The first formless realm, entered by absorption on boundless space after the perceptions of form have been transcended.
significance: Twenty thousand mahākappas — longer than any plane of the fine-material world — marks the threshold where form drops away entirely.

name: Ministers of Brahmā
description: The middle first-jhāna plane, whose beings serve as the retinue-ministers of Mahā-brahmā, with a lifespan given as half an incalculable aeon.
significance: Half of an uncounted quantity remains uncounted; the figure shown is a proportion of a phase, not a measured duration.

name: Neither-Perception-nor-Non-Perception
description: The highest of the four formless realms, reached through the fourth formless attainment, where perception is so subtle that it is called neither present nor absent.
significance: A lifespan of 84,000 mahākappas is the longest the cosmology states, and it still ends. The summit of saṃsāra is not liberation.

name: Non-Percipient Beings
description: A fine-material plane where beings exist with body alone, perception having been set aside through a particular fourth-jhāna attainment.
significance: Five hundred mahākappas pass without perception, which the tradition treats as attainment spent rather than put to use.

name: Nothingness
description: The third formless realm, attained through the absorption resting on the perception that there is nothing whatever.
significance: Sixty thousand world-cycles of refined absorption, in a plane the tradition holds to be without the conditions in which the Dhamma can be heard or taught.

name: Peerless Devas
description: The highest of the five Pure Abodes within the fine-material world, reached only by non-returners established in the fourth jhāna.
significance: As the highest of the Pure Abodes it admits no further rebirth beyond it, so its 16,000 mahākappas are a last span rather than another turn of the wheel.

name: Retinue of Brahmā
description: The lowest first-jhāna plane, whose lifespan is given as a third of an incalculable aeon.
significance: That the tradition speaks of thirds and halves of something it calls incalculable indicates these are structural ratios rather than arithmetic.

name: The Animal Realm
description: The woeful plane of animals, arising from delusion and the absence of virtue, with no fixed lifespan.
significance: The one lower realm visible to us, and the one whose lifespans vary so widely that the texts give no single figure.

name: The Contented Devas
description: The fourth sense-sphere heaven, where every Bodhisatta dwells awaiting his final birth, with a lifespan of 4,000 celestial years — given as 576,000,000 human years.
significance: The tradition holds that Metteyya abides here now, which gives this heaven a direct bearing on the future of the human plane. The plotted span is an ordering placeholder.

name: The Hells
description: The lowest plane, the result of grave unwholesome deeds, with no fixed lifespan — immense, the texts say, but never eternal.
significance: The duration is unfixed and always finite: no state in this cosmology, however terrible, lies beyond exhaustion.

name: The Present Moment
description: Now — roughly two and a half millennia after Gotama's passing, with his teaching still extant and a human birth still available.
significance: The texts treat this conjunction as rare: a Buddha has arisen, the Dhamma is still taught, and one is born where it can be heard.

name: The Thirty-Three Gods
description: Sakka's heaven on the summit of Mount Meru, where beings live 1,000 celestial years — given as 36,000,000 human years.
significance: The commentaries recount that the Buddha taught the Abhidhamma to his mother here, setting a celestial lifespan alongside a single human episode. The plotted value is a placeholder.

name: The Yāma Devas
description: The third sense-sphere heaven, whose beings live 2,000 celestial years — given as 144,000,000 human years.
significance: Each ascent through the six sensual heavens multiplies the stated lifespan fourfold, and the whole series still remains a fraction of one mahākappa.
note: Given in the texts as celestial years. Placing it on a kappa axis assumes a year-length for a kappa that the canon does not supply, so read its width as indicative only.

name: Titans / Anti-Gods
description: A woeful plane of beings driven by envy and conflict, whose lifespan the texts do not fix.
significance: No duration is stated; the plane lasts as long as the kamma that sustains it, so no endpoint can be plotted.

name: Untroubled Devas
description: The second of the Pure Abodes, whose inhabitants are free of distress and are all non-returners.
significance: Two thousand mahākappas without affliction is still a span to be exhausted, not a resting place.

name: Very Fruitful Devas
description: The ordinary fourth-jhāna plane of the fine-material world, outside the Pure Abodes.
significance: The Visuddhimagga places it above the reach of all three destructions — fire, water and wind — so it marks where world-destruction stops.

name: The Fall to Ten Years
description: The long moral and demographic decline in which, as generosity and restraint fail, the human lifespan drops by degrees until people live only ten years and a girl of five is of marriageable age.
significance: The decline ends in a sword-interval of mutual slaughter, after which the few who hide in forests and caves resolve to abstain from killing, and the lifespan begins to lengthen again.

name: Metteyya, the Buddha to Come
description: The bodhisatta Nātha, waiting now in the Tusita heaven, who will arise at Ketumatī (the future Bārāṇasī) when the human lifespan has climbed back to about 80,000 years, live 80,000 years, and teach an age of ease said to last 180,000 to 380,000 years.
significance: He is the only one of the ten whose life the Chronicles narrate in full — including his past life as the Universal Monarch Saṅkha of Indapatta, who on merely hearing the word 'Buddha' gave away his empire and set out on foot toward the Buddha Sirimata, walking until his feet split and bled and crawling on hands and knees by the fourth day — and he is the fifth and last Buddha of this present aeon.

name: The Rise to Eighty Thousand Years
description: The upward half of the cycle, in which renewed virtue lengthens the human lifespan generation by generation from ten years back to eighty thousand.
significance: Metteyya arises at the crest of this ascent, when the lifespan has returned to eighty thousand years; the sutta gives no count of years for his coming.

name: The Vanishing of the Dhamma
description: The predicted disappearance of the teaching in stages — attainment, then practice, then learning, then the outward forms, and finally the relics.
significance: The Dhamma is itself impermanent, and its loss is the condition under which a future Buddha must arise.

name: Rāma
description: A king, named second in the verse listing the ten future Buddhas, for whom the Chronicles give no elaborated life; his fuller account belongs to the Dasabodhisatta texts the Chronicles cite but do not reproduce.
significance: The texts fix no interval between one future Buddha and the next. His position here marks order in a sequence only; the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline, not a measure drawn from the sources.

name: The Dissolution of This World-System
description: The contraction and destruction of the present world-system after the last of its five Buddhas has come and gone, followed by the long emptiness before it expands again.
significance: The aeon ends as it began, and the world-system itself is shown to be as impermanent as anything within it.

name: Pasenadi of Kosala
description: The powerful king of Kosala who reigned at Sāvatthī during Gotama's lifetime, a devoted lay supporter deeply tied to the Jetavana community and one of the Buddha's foremost royal patrons.
significance: That a near-contemporary of Gotama, a man of his own court, stands third among the Buddhas yet to come shows the tradition placing the ripening of Awakening across spans no single life can hold. The texts fix no interval before he arises; the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.

name: Abhibhū
description: A celestial being, glossed in the verse as 'the Deva Abhibhū' and named there only; his fuller account belongs to the Dasabodhisatta texts rather than to the Chronicles.
significance: The name is shared by others in the tradition — a chief disciple of the past Buddha Sikhī, and a monk renowned for a voice that carried across a thousand world-systems — so the Chronicles leave this future Buddha identified by name alone. No interval is given for his arising; the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.

name: Dīghasoṇī
description: An asura lord — a titan of the anti-god realms — named only in the verse in this source, his elaborated life preserved elsewhere in the Dasabodhisatta texts.
significance: That a being of the asura realm is bound for Buddhahood shows that the vow is not confined to the human world. No interval is given for when he will arise; the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.

name: Caṇḍanī
description: A brahmin, named only in the verse in this source, with his elaborated future-Buddhahood preserved in the Dasabodhisatta texts the Chronicles reference.
significance: His placement here reflects sequence alone. The texts fix no measure of time between the ten, and the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.

name: Subha
description: A brahmin youth of Gotama's own time who, after the Buddha's passing, invited Ven. Ānanda for a meal and put to him a question on the doctrine; Ānanda's reply is preserved as the Discourse to Subha (Subha-sutta, DN 10).
significance: A seeker who questioned the teaching in Gotama's own generation is numbered among the Buddhas to come. The texts decline to measure the remove, and the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.

name: Todeyya
description: A wealthy brahmin of Tudigāma, father of Subha, named in the verse as a brahmin; a widely known commentarial story holds that through miserliness he was reborn as a dog in his own former house.
significance: He and his son are the only parent-and-child pair among the ten, and the commentarial story is a reminder that even a hard rebirth does not close the long road to Awakening — a road whose intervals the texts never number. The spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.

name: Nāḷāgiri the Elephant
description: The royal war-elephant of Rājagaha, whom Devadatta had made drunk and loosed in the streets to trample the Buddha, and who stopped and knelt at his feet when met with pure loving-kindness (mettā).
significance: That the very animal sent to kill the Buddha is himself destined for Buddhahood is the tradition's plainest statement of how wide the door stands open. The texts fix no interval before his arising; the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.

name: Pālaleyya the Elephant
description: A bull-elephant who attended the Buddha in solitude — clearing the ground, bringing water, and guarding his rest — when the Buddha withdrew from the quarrelling monks of Kosambī to the Rakkhita grove near Pāḷileyyaka village.
significance: He closes the line of ten as a servant-beast who kept the Buddha company where quarrelsome men could not. The texts name the order of the ten but nowhere fix the intervals between their arisings; the spacing shown is a convention of this timeline.
